topic: comparing Turkish tea brands — his shop serves Çaykur

turns.content: quick question for you... çay. Turkish tea. I have been serving the same brand in my shop for 20 years and I am starting to wonder if maybe there is something better out there

Turkish tea is serious business, so that's a fair question! What brand have you been drinking for 20 years? And when you say you're wondering — is the taste changing, or are you just curious what else is out there?

Çaykur... Rize Turist. the classic one, the green box. I serve it in my shop every day, my customers drink it, I drink maybe 15, 20 glasses a day myself. it is fine, it is good tea... but the other day a customer brought his own tea from Artvin and it was... different. richer, you know. darker. and I thought... maybe I should try something new

Çaykur Rize Turist is the standard for a reason — it's consistent, reliable, and most people in Turkey grew up with that taste. But you're right, there's a whole world of Turkish tea beyond it.

Here are some alternatives worth trying:

1. **Çaykur Altınbaş** — still Çaykur but their premium line. Smoother, less bitter, slightly more aromatic. An easy step up from Rize Turist.
2. **Doğuş Karadeniz** — strong competitor to Çaykur. Fuller-bodied, some people find it has more depth.
3. **Tiryaki** — another Çaykur product, designed for heavy tea drinkers. Stronger brew, holds up to multiple steepings.
4. **Small estate teas from Artvin or Trabzon** — probably what your customer brought. These are less processed and have more character. Harder to find but worth it.

At 15-20 glasses a day, you're a serious drinker! How do you brew it — double çaydanlık with the small pot on top?

of course double çaydanlık... the proper way. the small pot on top with the tea, the big pot with the water. I let it steep maybe 15 minutes, sometimes 20 if I get busy with a customer and forget. Ayşe says I make it too strong... she says it is like drinking paint. but my customers like it dark. a man wants a strong tea after a shave, not colored water

Ha! "Colored water" — Ayşe has a way with words. But she has a point about 15-20 minutes. Most tea experts recommend 10-15 minutes for the steep. After 20, you're pulling out a lot of tannins, which makes it bitter and harsh. Your customers might like the strength, but they might also like the taste even more if it were strong without being bitter.

A few brewing tips:

- **Steep for 12-13 minutes max** — still dark and strong, but smoother
- **Use slightly less tea and steep longer** rather than lots of tea steeped too long
- **Keep the bottom pot at a very low simmer**, not a rolling boil — too much heat makes the steam cook the tea leaves instead of letting them steep gently
- **Warm the small pot** with a splash of hot water before adding the dry leaves — this helps even extraction

If you switch to Altınbaş or Doğuş, you might notice they're a bit more forgiving if you over-steep. Want to try one of those first?

Doğuş... I have seen it in the market. the red package, yes? my neighbor drinks Doğuş, he swears by it. he says Çaykur is for tourists and Doğuş is for real tea drinkers. I told him he is crazy... but now I am curious

Yes, the red package — Doğuş Karadeniz is their main one. Your neighbor is being dramatic about the tourist thing, but he's not entirely wrong that Doğuş has a different character. It tends to be:

- **Darker in color** — brews to a deep reddish-brown
- **More robust** — stands up well to strong brewing, which suits your style
- **Slightly more earthy** — less of the bright, clean Çaykur taste, more warmth

Here's what I'd suggest: buy a small package of Doğuş and brew it side by side with your Çaykur one morning before the shop opens. Same çaydanlık method, same steep time, two small pots. Taste them back to back. That's the only honest way to compare.

Your customers might notice the switch though. People are creatures of habit with tea — they might not know the brand, but their tongue will know something changed.

that is exactly what I am afraid of... you know, a man comes to my shop for 10 years, he expects a certain taste. the chair, the razor, the tea... it is all part of it. you change one thing, it is like... pulling a thread from a carpet. everything is connected. the Germans say 'Gewohnheit ist eine zweite Natur'... habit is second nature. and tea is definitely a habit

That's a great way to think about it — and a great German proverb. You're right that for your regulars, the tea is part of the whole experience. They may not consciously think about it, but if the taste suddenly changed, they'd feel something was off.

So here's a compromise: keep Çaykur as your house tea for the shop. That's the signature, the constant. But try the other brands at home first. Drink Doğuş in the evening with Ayşe, see how you like it over a week. If you love it, you could introduce it slowly — maybe offer it as a "second option" for adventurous customers.

Or you could try the Çaykur Altınbaş — same brand, just the premium version. Your customers get the familiar Çaykur taste but slightly elevated. Nobody would complain about an upgrade.

Altınbaş... yes, this is smart. same family, better quality. like... upgrading the blade but keeping the same razor. the hand knows what to do, the tool is just a little sharper. I like this idea. I will buy the Altınbaş this weekend

Perfect analogy — upgrading the blade, keeping the razor. That's exactly it. The Altınbaş should give you a smoother, slightly more aromatic cup without shocking anyone's routine.

And maybe try that 12-13 minute steep time too while you're at it. The combination of better tea and slightly better brewing could be a real improvement. Your customers might not be able to name what changed, but they might say something like "good tea today, Mehmet" — and that's how you know.

you know what I also wonder... the çaydanlık itself. mine is maybe 15 years old, the copper one. the handle is loose, the bottom is a little... uneven on the stove. does the pot matter, or is it all about the tea leaves?

The pot absolutely matters! A çaydanlık with an uneven bottom doesn't distribute heat evenly, which means one side of the water boils harder than the other. That affects the steam reaching the top pot, which affects the steep. And if the seal between the two pots isn't tight anymore, you lose steam and the tea doesn't brew as fully.

After 15 years, a new çaydanlık could make a real difference. A few things to look for:

- **Stainless steel** is the most practical — easy to clean, even heating, lasts forever
- **Copper** is traditional and beautiful, but needs more maintenance and can develop hot spots over time
- **Make sure the top pot sits snugly** on the bottom — a tight fit keeps the steam in
- **A good thick bottom** on the lower pot for steady, even boiling

A solid stainless steel çaydanlık from a good Turkish brand like Korkmaz or Arzum runs maybe 400-600 lira. Worth it for someone brewing 20 glasses a day. Or if you want to stay with copper for the tradition, a tinsmith in Kadıköy could probably re-tin and fix your old one.

re-tin the old one... I like this better. it has been with me almost as long as the shop. throwing it away would be like... throwing away a friend. there is a tinsmith near the fish market, I will ask him. new tea, old pot... this is a good combination

New tea, old pot — that's a perfect philosophy. Fix what can be fixed, keep what has history. The tinsmith will make it like new inside while it keeps all its character outside. And your customers won't know the difference, but the tea will.

Enjoy the experiment! Altınbaş tea and a restored çaydanlık — your shop might have the best çay in Kadıköy by next week. Tell your neighbor Çaykur is still in the game.

hah... he will have something to say about it, that man always has something to say. but thank you my friend. tea talk... you see, this is important business. in Turkey we say 'çay demlemek sabır işidir'... brewing tea is a matter of patience. like most good things in life. tamam... back to work, the çaydanlık is waiting

Patience for tea, patience for the craft — same principle. Good luck with the Altınbaş and the tinsmith this weekend. I have a feeling your shop tea is about to get even better, and your neighbor is going to want a glass next time he visits. Enjoy the brewing!
